entries.title: Project files: temporary versus disk-based
entries.body:
# Project files - where Burp stores your work

Every time you launch Burp, the first thing it asks is: which project to work in. A project is a container for everything Burp accumulates during a session: **Proxy** history, the site map, **Repeater** tabs, scanner findings, settings. Whether your work survives closing the program depends on which project type you choose at startup.

The startup wizard offers these options:

- **Temporary project** - a temporary project entirely in memory.
- **New project on disk** (Professional only) - a new project saved to a file on disk.
- **Open existing project** (Professional only) - open a previously saved project file.

```
Welcome to Burp Suite Professional
[*] Temporary project
[ ] New project on disk        <- name + file
[ ] Open existing project      <- list of recent
        [Next] -> configuration choice -> [Start Burp]
```

## Temporary project (in memory)

A **Temporary project** keeps ALL data in RAM. As soon as you close Burp, everything is gone: history, **Repeater** tabs, findings. You cannot save such a project "as is" to a file with a single click - but you can make a copy (see **Project > Save copy** below) and thereby turn it into a disk-based one.

When to use a temporary project:

- a quick one-off check, decoding a token, running a single request through **Repeater**;
- you do not mind losing the data;
- you are on **Community Edition** - there it is the only available option at all.

> Pitfall: memory. A temporary project is limited by the amount of RAM allocated to Burp. Launch a large **Crawl and audit** or wander a heavy site for a long time, and you will hit the memory ceiling, Burp will start lagging or crash, and all unsaved work will vanish. For long engagements this is not an option.

## Disk-based project (on disk)

**New project on disk** creates a project file into which Burp writes data INCREMENTALLY as you work. You do not need to press "save" separately - Burp appends to the file itself, in real time. If the program crashes, almost everything you managed to produce will remain in the file.

What is stored in the project file:

- all intercepted traffic and the **Proxy > HTTP history**;
- the site map **Target > Site map**;
- **Repeater** and **Intruder** tabs and their payloads;
- scan results and findings (Pro);
- project-level settings (project options) - scope, session handling, etc.

When to use a disk-based project:

- any serious engagement or multi-day hunting;
- a large application where there will be a lot of data;
- you need to be able to close Burp and continue tomorrow from the same place.

> Tip: for disk-based projects keep the file on a LOCAL drive. Burp's incremental write mechanism does not play well with network shares and cloud folders (the same OneDrive/Dropbox) - lag and file corruption are possible. It is best to disable syncing of the project folder while you work.

## Community versus Professional

The difference is fundamental:

- **Professional** - both types: temporary and disk-based projects. You can save, open, and import.
- **Community Edition** - ONLY **Temporary project**. You cannot save work to a file the regular way; close Burp and you lose the session. This is one of the key limitations of the free version.

## Trusting other people's files

A project file carries not only data but also settings that can trigger activity. That is why opening a file offers two protective toggles:

- **Pause automated tasks** - open the project with automated tasks paused, so that nobody else's scan blasts off into the network right after opening.
- **Trust this project file** - mark the file as trusted; if you do not trust it, Burp will scrub potentially dangerous settings out of it.

> Pitfall: do not open other people's .burp files thoughtlessly. The project settings may have things written in them that will start sending requests outward upon opening. Open other people's files as untrusted and with tasks paused.

## File size

Testing some applications generates A LOT of data - the project file easily grows to several gigabytes. Before a long engagement, make sure there is spare free space on disk, otherwise at some point Burp will not be able to append to the file.

entries.title: Creating a project file on disk
entries.body:
# Creating a disk-based project (Professional)

You can create a project file only in **Burp Suite Professional** - in **Community Edition** this option does not exist. The process goes through the startup wizard and takes literally half a minute.

## Steps

1. Launch Burp Suite. The startup screen will appear with a choice of project type.
2. Choose **New project on disk**.
3. In the **Name** field, enter a meaningful project name (for example, the target's name or the engagement number).
4. In the **File** field, specify where to save the file. You can type the path by hand or click **Choose file** and select the folder/name through a dialog.
5. Click **Next**.
6. Choose which configuration to apply (see below).
7. Click **Start Burp** - Burp will open already bound to the work file.

```
New project on disk
  Name: [ acme-bugbounty            ]
  File: [ C:\burp\acme.burp         ] [Choose file]
        [Next]
```

## Choosing a configuration

At the wizard's second step, Burp asks where to get the settings for the new project from:

- **Use Burp defaults** - standard out-of-the-box settings. The usual choice for a new project.
- **Use settings saved with project** - available only when OPENING an existing project; restores the settings saved inside the file.
- **Load from configuration file** - pull settings from a previously saved Burp configuration file. Note: only PROJECT-level settings are applied; user-level ones remain your current ones.

> Tip: if you have a ready configuration for a certain type of target (your own set of scope rules, a scanner profile, session handling), save it as a separate file and attach it via **Load from configuration file** when creating each new project - you will not have to set things up from scratch.

## Where to save the file

Choose a LOCAL drive. Because of how Burp incrementally appends data, network drives and synced cloud folders (OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive) can lead to lag and file corruption. It is best to keep the project folder outside the sync zone.

> Pitfall: disk space. Plan ahead for the fact that a large project can take several gigabytes. Creating the file on an almost full partition is a sure path to Burp being unable to append data in the middle of a scan.

## After creation

No manual "saving" is required afterward - Burp writes to the file as you work. When you want to continue later, at startup choose **Open existing project** and point to this file (or pick it from the recent list).

entries.title: Managing project files
entries.body:
# Managing project files

Once a disk-based project is created, Burp maintains it itself: data is appended to the file incrementally, in real time, with no need to press "save". But sometimes you need to make a copy, hand the project to another person, or recover a corrupted file - for this there are separate operations in the **Project** menu.

## Saving a copy (Save copy)

The **Project > Save copy** menu makes a copy of the current project into a new file. This is also the way to turn a TEMPORARY project into a disk-based one: open the temporary one, do **Save copy**, and from then on Burp writes to the file.

When saving a copy you can flexibly choose what exactly to put in the file:

- which tools to include (**Proxy** history, **Repeater**, etc.);
- **Save in-scope items only** - save only what falls within scope, so as not to drag along extra junk;
- whether to include **Dashboard** data;
- whether to keep **Burp Collaborator** identifiers.

Burp lets you keep working while the copy is saved in the background, although actions on the data being saved at that moment may lag a little.

> Tip: for long projects, **Save copy** with the **Save in-scope items only** box checked is handy as a way to make a "clean" compact slice for a report or handoff - without tons of irrelevant traffic.

## Importing someone else's project (Import project file)

The **Project > Import project file** menu lets you MERGE another project file into the current disk-based project - the data of the two projects is combined. Handy when you need to gather in one place the results obtained on different machines or by different people.

When importing, the same protective toggles are available as when opening:

- **Pause automated tasks** - merge with tasks paused;
- whether to trust the imported file.

> Pitfall: import files ONLY from trusted sources. Someone else's project may carry activity in its settings that will start sending requests after import. If the source is not 100% your own - import with tasks paused and as untrusted.

## Recovering a corrupted file

If, on opening, Burp detects that the project file is corrupted (for example, after a sudden power loss or killing the process), it automatically offers to run the recovery tool:

1. You specify where to save the recovered data.
2. You click **Recover data**.
3. When ready, you click **Open recovered data file**.

The tool pulls as much data out of the corrupted file as it can - but nobody guarantees a complete recovery.

> Pitfall: treat the recovered file as read-only. If you keep actively working right in it, the risk of new crashes and data loss grows. It is more correct to open the recovered data, immediately make a fresh healthy project via **Save copy**, and continue working in that one.

## When Collections are better than the whole project

If you need to share a small set of requests/responses, you do not have to shuttle the whole multi-gigabyte project file back and forth - for that there are **Collections** (sets of messages). This is safer and lighter than dragging an entire project with all its settings.

> Tip: when handing over a project or when running several Burp instances at the same time, remove the **Collaborator** identifiers from the saved copy - otherwise conflicts and confusion in interactions are possible.
